source: Epicurus — Principal Doctrines and Letter to Menoeceus (Public Domain, Gutenberg 67707)

maxims.text: A blessed and indestructible being has no trouble himself and brings no trouble upon any other being.
Death is nothing to us, since when we exist, death is not yet present, and when death is present, then we do not exist.
The magnitude of pleasure reaches its limit in the removal of all pain.
Pain does not last continuously in the flesh, but the acutest pain is there for a very short time.
It is not possible to live pleasantly without living prudently and honourably and justly.
The natural wealth of a man is bounded and easily obtained; the wealth of vain fancies falls short of no bound.
Justice is a contract not to harm or be harmed, made among those who have the power to do so.
The soul does not permit the body to grieve when it has no share in that grief.
Among the things held to be just by law, whatever is proved to be of advantage in the requirements of men's dealings with one another has the stamp of justice.
The wise man is not more disturbed by himself than by fortune.
Among external goods the greatest is the possession of friends.
Of all the means which wisdom acquires to ensure happiness throughout the whole of life, by far the most important is the possession of friendship.

leaf_words.note: hedone — the aim, not the starting point
ataraxia — the final state of the mind

ataraxia_description: A glass sphere, perfectly empty. Not hollow — empty: the glass itself is the boundary of a contained absence.
letter_fragment: Do not spoil what you have by desiring what you have not; remember that what you now have was once among the things you only hoped for.

llm_prompts.garden_weight_0: The garden is perfectly still. The protagonist moves through it without adding to it.
llm_prompts.garden_weight_1_2: The garden presses slightly. The weight of choices taken is present but not yet crushing.
llm_prompts.garden_weight_3plus: The garden is a burden. Every object the protagonist carries is a small argument against peace.
